segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Listen to a talk on a marine biology podcast.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: There are many species out there that we wouldn't expect to see flying.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Mammals, reptiles, one fascinating creature is the flying squid.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Now squid have fins, arms, tentacles and gills for breathing.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Their main body, the mantle, is basically a tube of muscle that water goes inside of while they swim.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: When this mantle clamps shut, it pumps the water out, which propels the squid through water quite quickly.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: And a flying squid are able to propel themselves right up through the ocean's surface and into the air above.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Another unexpected flying creature is the flying squirrel, which can simply glide sometimes quickly and for long distances between tree branches.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: However, unlike the flying squirrel, flying squid not only glide but can actually propel themselves upward.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: And, well, a flying squid can accelerate as fast as a car, getting up to 100 kilometers per hour in about a second.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Then the squid extends its tentacles and arms, and it has membranes between them that spread out and help the squid glide for several seconds.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: A squid in flight is likely to be fleeing from an underwater predator or a ship.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: There are some alternative theories though.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: One is that it's a migration strategy, since moving through the air takes less energy and time than moving through water.
